title: Microplastics: An Invisible Threat
summary: An examination of how microplastics have spread throughout every environment on Earth and what science is learning about their effects on living organisms.
body: Every time you open a bottle of mineral water, wash a synthetic fleece jacket, or drive a car along a busy road, tiny particles of plastic are released into the environment. These particles, known as microplastics, measure less than five millimetres in length, and the smallest of them — nanoplastics — are invisible to the naked eye. Over the past two decades, scientists have found them in places that once seemed impossibly remote: in the ice of the Antarctic, in the tissues of deep-sea fish, in the air above the Pyrenees mountains, and most recently inside the human bloodstream and lung tissue. The scale of contamination has startled even the researchers who study it. Microplastics originate from two sources. Primary microplastics are manufactured at a small size for specific purposes — the plastic beads once added to exfoliating cosmetics, for example, or the microfibre pellets shed by synthetic clothing during machine washing. Secondary microplastics form when larger plastic objects break down under ultraviolet radiation, physical abrasion, and chemical weathering. A plastic bag discarded in the ocean does not simply disappear; it fragments over years into progressively smaller pieces, each fragment eventually small enough to enter the food chain. The biological consequences of this contamination are still being investigated, but early findings are concerning. Laboratory studies have shown that microplastics can disrupt the hormonal systems of fish, reduce fertility in some marine invertebrates, and carry toxic chemical additives that leach into tissues. In humans, the full picture remains unclear. Microplastics have been detected in placental tissue, in the lungs of people living in urban areas, and in arterial plaques, though establishing a direct causal link between their presence and specific diseases remains methodologically difficult. What scientists do agree on is that the problem is now global and self-perpetuating. Plastic production worldwide has increased roughly twentyfold since the 1960s, and current recycling infrastructure cannot keep pace. Much of the plastic produced each year is single-use packaging that enters the waste stream almost immediately. Even plastic that is correctly disposed of can escape into the environment through landfill leachate, storm-water runoff, and the wind. Reducing the production of plastics at source — not merely improving waste management — is increasingly seen as the only realistic long-term solution. Some countries have already banned single-use plastics such as cotton buds, cutlery, and polystyrene cups. But critics argue that voluntary measures and national bans are insufficient given the transboundary nature of plastic pollution: particles released in one country can be detected in another within weeks. An international treaty on plastic pollution is currently being negotiated, though reaching agreement on binding targets has proved contentious. In the meantime, microplastics continue to accumulate in soils, waterways, and organisms at a rate that outpaces our scientific understanding of their long-term impact.

annotations.exampleEn: Washing a synthetic fleece releases thousands of plastic microfibres.
Nanoplastics are too small to be seen with the naked eye.
The scale of contamination in the river shocked local authorities.
Primary microplastics include the microbeads found in facial scrubs.
Ultraviolet radiation from the sun breaks down plastic over time.
Physical abrasion from waves helps fragment plastic debris.
Chemical weathering of plastic releases harmful additives.
Microplastics enter the food chain when fish consume contaminated water.
Some chemicals disrupt the hormonal systems of wildlife.

annotations.meaningVi: động vật không xương sống biển
annotations.exampleEn: Marine invertebrates such as mussels are filter feeders vulnerable to microplastics.
Toxic additives can leach into tissues from ingested plastic particles.
Microplastics have been found in placental tissue, raising health concerns.
Researchers found microplastic particles within arterial plaques.
Establishing a causal link between pollution and disease requires large-scale studies.

annotations.meaningVi: tự duy trì, tự nhân rộng
annotations.exampleEn: Plastic pollution is self-perpetuating because it keeps fragmenting into smaller pieces.
Much single-use packaging ends up in landfill within days of purchase.

annotations.meaningVi: nước rỉ từ bãi chôn lấp
annotations.exampleEn: Landfill leachate can carry plastic particles into groundwater.
Air pollution has a transboundary nature that makes national regulation alone insufficient.
Negotiators struggled to agree on binding targets for emissions reductions.

grammarNotes.title: Passive Constructions in Scientific Writing
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong văn bản khoa học, câu bị động rất phổ biến vì chúng nhấn mạnh vào đối tượng nghiên cứu thay vì người thực hiện. Trong bài này, các ví dụ như 'Microplastics have been detected in placental tissue' và 'Microplastics have been found in places that once seemed impossibly remote' đều dùng bị động hoàn thành (present perfect passive: have/has been + past participle). Cấu trúc này được dùng khi người nói muốn nhấn mạnh kết quả hơn là ai đã thực hiện nghiên cứu.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The passive voice is extensively used in scientific texts to focus on what was discovered rather than who discovered it. Examples in this lesson include 'Microplastics have been detected in placental tissue' and 'Microplastics have been found in places that once seemed impossibly remote'. These are present perfect passive constructions (have/has been + past participle), used to report findings whose recency is relevant. Recognising this pattern helps learners understand academic and journalistic writing more fluently.

grammarNotes.title: Concessive Clauses with 'though' and 'even though'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Mệnh đề nhượng bộ được dùng để thể hiện sự tương phản hoặc nghịch lý. Trong bài, 'though establishing a direct causal link ... remains methodologically difficult' và 'though reaching agreement on binding targets has proved contentious' đều sử dụng 'though' đặt ở đầu mệnh đề phụ. Lưu ý rằng 'though' có thể đứng ở đầu câu hoặc ở cuối câu (informal), trong khi 'even though' thường mạnh hơn và thường đứng ở đầu mệnh đề. Ở trình độ B2, người học cần phân biệt được các mệnh đề nhượng bộ này với các liên từ đơn giản hơn như 'but' hoặc 'however'.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Concessive clauses express contrast or concession — the idea that something is true despite another fact. In this lesson, 'though establishing a direct causal link remains methodologically difficult' and 'though reaching agreement on binding targets has proved contentious' use 'though' to introduce a concessive clause. 'Though' can appear at the start or end of a clause (informal). 'Even though' is stronger and more emphatic. At B2 level, producing these structures accurately — rather than falling back on 'but' — marks a significant step in writing and speaking sophistication.

translationVi: Mỗi lần bạn mở một chai nước khoáng, giặt một chiếc áo lông cừu tổng hợp, hay lái xe trên một con đường đông đúc, những hạt nhựa nhỏ xíu được giải phóng vào môi trường. Những hạt này, được gọi là vi nhựa, có kích thước nhỏ hơn năm milimet, và những hạt nhỏ nhất trong số chúng — nano nhựa — không thể nhìn thấy bằng mắt thường. Trong hai thập kỷ qua, các nhà khoa học đã tìm thấy chúng ở những nơi từng có vẻ hoàn toàn xa xôi: trong băng của Nam Cực, trong mô của cá biển sâu, trong không khí trên dãy núi Pyrenees, và gần đây nhất trong máu và mô phổi của con người. Quy mô ô nhiễm đã khiến ngay cả các nhà nghiên cứu nghiên cứu nó cũng phải kinh ngạc. Vi nhựa có nguồn gốc từ hai nguồn. Vi nhựa sơ cấp được sản xuất ở kích thước nhỏ cho các mục đích cụ thể — chẳng hạn như các hạt nhựa từng được thêm vào mỹ phẩm tẩy tế bào chết, hay các hạt vi sợi bị thoát ra từ quần áo tổng hợp khi giặt bằng máy. Vi nhựa thứ cấp hình thành khi các vật thể nhựa lớn hơn bị phân hủy dưới bức xạ tia cực tím, mài mòn vật lý và phong hóa hóa học. Một chiếc túi nhựa bị vứt xuống đại dương không đơn giản biến mất; nó vỡ vụn theo nhiều năm thành những mảnh ngày càng nhỏ hơn, mỗi mảnh cuối cùng đủ nhỏ để xâm nhập vào chuỗi thức ăn. Hậu quả sinh học của sự ô nhiễm này vẫn đang được điều tra, nhưng những phát hiện ban đầu đáng lo ngại. Các nghiên cứu trong phòng thí nghiệm đã chỉ ra rằng vi nhựa có thể phá vỡ hệ thống nội tiết tố của cá, làm giảm khả năng sinh sản ở một số động vật không xương sống biển, và mang các chất phụ gia hóa học độc hại thấm vào mô. Ở người, bức tranh toàn cảnh vẫn chưa rõ ràng. Vi nhựa đã được phát hiện trong mô nhau thai, trong phổi của những người sống ở các khu vực đô thị, và trong các mảng bám động mạch, mặc dù việc thiết lập mối liên hệ nhân quả trực tiếp giữa sự hiện diện của chúng và các bệnh cụ thể vẫn còn khó khăn về mặt phương pháp. Điều mà các nhà khoa học đồng ý là vấn đề này hiện đã có tính toàn cầu và tự duy trì. Sản xuất nhựa trên toàn thế giới đã tăng khoảng hai mươi lần kể từ những năm 1960, và cơ sở hạ tầng tái chế hiện tại không thể theo kịp. Phần lớn nhựa được sản xuất mỗi năm là bao bì dùng một lần, đi vào luồng chất thải gần như ngay lập tức. Ngay cả nhựa được xử lý đúng cách cũng có thể thoát vào môi trường qua nước rỉ từ bãi chôn lấp, dòng chảy nước mưa và gió. Giảm sản xuất nhựa từ nguồn gốc — không chỉ đơn thuần cải thiện quản lý chất thải — ngày càng được coi là giải pháp dài hạn thực tế duy nhất. Một số quốc gia đã cấm nhựa dùng một lần như tăm bông, đồ dùng ăn uống và cốc xốp. Nhưng các nhà phê bình lập luận rằng các biện pháp tự nguyện và lệnh cấm quốc gia là không đủ khi xét đến tính chất xuyên biên giới của ô nhiễm nhựa: các hạt được giải phóng ở một quốc gia có thể được phát hiện ở quốc gia khác trong vòng vài tuần. Một hiệp ước quốc tế về ô nhiễm nhựa hiện đang được đàm phán, mặc dù việc đạt được thỏa thuận về các mục tiêu ràng buộc đã được chứng minh là gây tranh cãi. Trong thời gian chờ đợi, vi nhựa tiếp tục tích tụ trong đất, đường thủy và sinh vật với tốc độ vượt quá sự hiểu biết khoa học của chúng ta về tác động lâu dài của chúng.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is given as an everyday source of microplastics?
questions.explanation: The passage mentions washing a synthetic fleece jacket as one everyday activity that releases microplastic particles.
questions.hint: Look at the very first sentence of the passage.

questions.prompt: What distinguishes nanoplastics from other microplastics?
options: They are made from a different type of polymer
They only form in ocean water
They are invisible to the naked eye
They are produced intentionally in factories
questions.explanation: The passage says nanoplastics 'are invisible to the naked eye'.
questions.hint: The answer is in the opening paragraph.

questions.prompt: Where have microplastics been found that once seemed impossibly remote?
options: In rainwater in major cities only
In the Antarctic ice and the human bloodstream
In the stomachs of domestic cats
questions.explanation: The passage lists Antarctic ice, deep-sea fish tissues, mountain air, the human bloodstream, and lung tissue as locations once considered remote.
questions.hint: Multiple remote locations are listed in the first paragraph.

options: Microplastics manufactured at a small size for specific purposes
Fragments from the natural breakdown of large plastic items
Plastics produced in countries with few environmental regulations
questions.explanation: Primary microplastics are defined as those 'manufactured at a small size for specific purposes'.
questions.hint: The passage distinguishes primary from secondary microplastics.

options: They are manufactured deliberately as beads in cosmetics
They are produced when plastic is recycled incorrectly
They form when larger plastic objects break down through radiation, abrasion, and weathering
They are released by burning plastic in open air
questions.explanation: Secondary microplastics form 'when larger plastic objects break down under ultraviolet radiation, physical abrasion, and chemical weathering'.
questions.hint: Look for the explanation of secondary microplastics.

questions.prompt: According to early laboratory findings, what effect can microplastics have on fish?
options: They improve growth rates by adding nutrients
They cause fish to avoid certain water depths
They have no measurable effect on fish biology
questions.explanation: Laboratory studies have shown that microplastics 'can disrupt the hormonal systems of fish'.
questions.hint: The answer is in the paragraph on biological consequences.

questions.prompt: What is methodologically difficult according to the passage?
options: Counting the number of plastic fragments in the ocean
Establishing a causal link between microplastics and specific human diseases
Banning single-use plastics in developed countries
questions.explanation: The passage states that 'establishing a direct causal link between their presence and specific diseases remains methodologically difficult'.
questions.hint: This relates to what scientists still cannot yet prove about human health.

questions.prompt: By roughly how much has plastic production increased since the 1960s?
questions.explanation: The passage states that plastic production 'has increased roughly twentyfold since the 1960s'.
questions.hint: A specific figure is given in the paragraph about the global scale of the problem.

questions.prompt: What is the passage's view on improving waste management alone?
options: It is the most effective solution to microplastic pollution
It is sufficient if all countries cooperate
It is not enough; reducing plastic production at source is also needed
It has no role to play in solving the problem
questions.explanation: The passage argues that 'reducing the production of plastics at source — not merely improving waste management — is increasingly seen as the only realistic long-term solution'.
questions.hint: Look for the contrast between waste management and source reduction.

questions.prompt: Which of the following single-use plastics is mentioned as having been banned in some countries?
options: Plastic bags used in grocery stores
questions.explanation: The passage mentions 'polystyrene cups' as one of the single-use plastics that some countries have banned.
questions.hint: Three specific examples of banned items are listed.

questions.prompt: Why do critics argue that national bans alone are insufficient?
options: Because they are too expensive to enforce effectively
Because plastic pollution is transboundary and particles can cross national borders quickly
Because consumers prefer plastic to alternative materials
Because manufacturers simply relocate production abroad
questions.explanation: Critics argue that national bans are insufficient 'given the transboundary nature of plastic pollution: particles released in one country can be detected in another within weeks'.

questions.prompt: What is currently being negotiated at the international level?
options: A global tax on plastic production
A treaty on plastic pollution with binding targets
An agreement to ban all single-use plastics globally by 2030
A fund to clean up the Great Pacific Garbage Patch
questions.explanation: The passage refers to 'an international treaty on plastic pollution' with binding targets that 'is currently being negotiated'.
questions.hint: This is mentioned near the end of the passage.

questions.prompt: What does the passage imply about microplastics in foods and drinks?
options: They are impossible to avoid in modern life
They only affect people who eat seafood regularly
They have been entirely removed from the food supply by filtration
They are found only in ocean fish, not in land animals
questions.explanation: The opening examples — bottled water, synthetic clothing, driving — imply that microplastic exposure is essentially unavoidable in modern life.
questions.hint: Consider what the everyday examples in the opening suggest.

questions.prompt: In what way does the passage describe plastic pollution as 'self-perpetuating'?
options: Because plastic is designed to last forever without degrading
Because people keep buying more plastic despite knowing the risks
Because plastic keeps fragmenting into smaller and smaller pieces
Because plastic production stimulates further investment in manufacturing
questions.explanation: The problem is self-perpetuating because plastic keeps fragmenting into progressively smaller pieces rather than disappearing.
questions.hint: Think about the physical process of plastic breakdown.

questions.prompt: What does the passage suggest about the relationship between scientific knowledge and the rate of microplastic accumulation?
options: Science has fully mapped all the effects of microplastics on ecosystems
Microplastics are accumulating faster than science can understand their long-term impact
Scientific knowledge has outpaced the growth of plastic pollution
Scientists disagree on whether microplastics actually accumulate in the environment
questions.explanation: The final sentence states that microplastics 'accumulate in soils, waterways, and organisms at a rate that outpaces our scientific understanding of their long-term impact'.
questions.hint: The closing sentence directly addresses this relationship.

cloze.template: Microplastics measure less than five millimetres and the smallest — nanoplastics — are invisible to the {{b1}}. Scientists have found them in Antarctic {{b2}}, deep-sea fish tissues, and even the human {{b3}}. Primary microplastics are {{b4}} at a small size for specific purposes, while secondary microplastics form when larger plastic objects {{b5}} under ultraviolet radiation and {{b6}}. Laboratory studies have shown that microplastics can disrupt the {{b7}} systems of fish and reduce {{b8}} in some marine invertebrates. Toxic chemical additives can {{b9}} into tissues. In humans, microplastics have been found in {{b10}} tissue and arterial {{b11}}, but establishing a direct {{b12}} link to specific diseases remains difficult. Plastic production has increased roughly {{b13}} since the 1960s. Much plastic is {{b14}} packaging that enters the waste stream almost immediately. Reducing plastic {{b15}} at source is seen as the only realistic long-term solution. National bans are considered insufficient because plastic pollution is {{b16}} in nature. An international {{b17}} on plastic pollution is currently being negotiated. Microplastics continue to {{b18}} in soils and waterways at a rate that outpaces scientific {{b19}} of their long-term {{b20}}.

blanks.explanation: Nanoplastics are 'invisible to the naked eye'.
Microplastics have been found 'in the ice of the Antarctic'.
Scientists found microplastics 'inside the human bloodstream and lung tissue'.
Primary microplastics are 'manufactured at a small size for specific purposes'.
Secondary microplastics form when larger objects 'break down' under UV radiation.
Breakdown occurs under 'ultraviolet radiation, physical abrasion, and chemical weathering'.
Microplastics 'can disrupt the hormonal systems of fish'.
They 'reduce fertility in some marine invertebrates'.
Toxic additives 'leach into tissues'.
Microplastics have been detected 'in placental tissue'.
Microplastics have been found 'in arterial plaques'.
A 'direct causal link' between microplastics and disease is hard to establish.
Plastic production 'has increased roughly twentyfold since the 1960s'.
Most problematic plastic is 'single-use packaging'.
Reducing 'plastic production at source' is seen as the long-term solution.
Plastic pollution 'has a transboundary nature'.
'An international treaty on plastic pollution is currently being negotiated'.
Microplastics 'continue to accumulate in soils, waterways, and organisms'.
The rate 'outpaces our scientific understanding'.
Science lags behind in understanding the 'long-term impact' of microplastics.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The passage argues that reducing plastic production at source is more effective than improving waste management. However, reducing production would have significant economic consequences for industries that rely on plastic packaging. How should societies balance the environmental need to cut plastic output against the economic and logistical dependence on plastics in global supply chains? And who — governments, corporations, or consumers — should bear the primary responsibility for driving this change?
